title: Your first decision
instruction: An `if` statement runs code only when a condition is true. Run this, then change `age` to 15 and run again to see what changes.
successMessage: When the condition is true, the code inside runs. When false, Go skips it entirely.

title: Comparison operators
instruction: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` — six operators. Change `age` to different values and see which lines print.
successMessage: Six operators. Every condition you'll ever write uses at least one.

title: if-else — two paths
instruction: `if` runs one path, `else` runs the other. Change `age` to 10, then to 25, and watch which branch runs each time.
successMessage: Two paths. One condition. Go picks the right one every time.

title: else if — more than two paths
instruction: `else if` adds more branches. Change `age` to 4, 9, 14, or 25 — each lands in a different library section.
hint: Change the age value and hit Run. Each age lands in a different branch.
successMessage: Go checks conditions in order and stops at the first true one. Order matters.

title: Logical operators — combine conditions
instruction: `&&` means AND (both must be true), `||` means OR (at least one). Try changing `hasLibraryCard` to `false` and run again.
expectedOutput: Visit the front desk for assistance
successMessage: && needs both. || needs one. ! flips it.

title: switch — membership tiers
instruction: `switch` checks one variable against many cases. Change `tier` to "Gold", "Silver", or "Bronze" and run each time.
hint: Change the tier := value to see different cases run.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	tier := "Gold"
	switch tier {
	case "Gold":
		fmt.Println("Unlimited books. No late fees.")
	case "Silver":
		fmt.Println("10 books at a time.")
	case "Bronze":
		fmt.Println("5 books at a time.")
	default:
		fmt.Println("Basic membership — 2 books at a time.")
	}
}
successMessage: switch is if-else but readable. The default case catches anything that didn't match.

title: Multiple values in one case
instruction: Several values can share one case: `case "Overdue", "Lost":`. Add a case that matches both and prints a fine message.
hint: case "Overdue", "Lost": — comma-separated values in one case line.
expectedOutput: Fine applies — visit the front desk

codeChecks.message: Add a case that matches both "Overdue" and "Lost" using a comma.
Print something about a fine or fee in your new case.
Replace the TODO comment with your case.

successMessage: One case, multiple values. No repetition. Go is sensible like that.

title: switch with no condition 🤯
instruction: A `switch` without a variable acts like a clean `if-else` chain. Run this, then try `daysOverdue := 7`.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	daysOverdue := 45
	switch {
	case daysOverdue >= 30:
		fmt.Println("Suspended borrowing privileges")
	case daysOverdue >= 14:
		fmt.Println("Overdue notice sent")
	case daysOverdue >= 1:
		fmt.Println("Gentle reminder — please return soon")
	default:
		fmt.Println("No overdue items. Thank you!")
	}
}
successMessage: switch without a variable is just a cleaner if-else. Same logic, easier to read.

title: Build a decision machine
instruction: Write a program with `age`, `itemsBorrowed`, and `hasMembership`. Print "Premium" if items >= 20 AND hasMembership, else "Regular" if items >= 5, else "Newcomer".
hint: Start with the strictest condition first (Premium requires both items AND membership).

codeChecks.message: Use at least one else if.
Print at least one of: Premium, Regular, or Newcomer.

successMessage: You built a program that thinks. Every app you've ever used is full of logic like this.
